title: Wet-felted basket tray
subtitle: A shallow wool tray with approximately 5 cm sides, shaped over a flat resist, finished size around 25 by 18 cm
excerpt: Wet-felt a shallow basket tray with low sides by working merino roving over a flat resist. The resist shape controls the tray floor and the side walls. Starting dimensions of 35 by 25 cm shrink to approximately 25 by 18 cm with 5 cm sides after fulling. The finished tray is firm enough to hold its shape in use and can store small objects on a desk or dressing table.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from the resist-felting tradition for shallow 3D forms. Flat-resist basket and tray construction is standard guild and workshop practice; the method is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre drawn into a long ribbon ready for felting. Thin wisps pulled from the end of the roving are laid in cross-hatched layers over and around the resist.

A flat template, typically cut from foam or bubble wrap, placed inside the layered wool to prevent the front and back faces from bonding together. In tray-making the resist defines the floor area; the roving folded over the edges forms the walls.

The vigorous agitation phase of wet felting that follows initial surface bonding. Rolling, rubbing, and throwing compact the fibres, shrink the piece to finished dimensions, and firm the walls of the tray into their upright shape.

content.text: A wet-felted basket tray is built over a flat foam
resist
cut to the planned floor area of the tray. Merino
roving

is laid over the resist and folded onto the reverse face to build up continuous wall depth. The resist prevents the top and bottom faces from bonding together across the floor area; the edges, where roving has been folded over without a resist barrier, bond first and form the walls. The resist is removed partway through
fulling

, allowing the walls to be shaped upright. Starting at 35 by 25 cm of wool coverage, the finished tray is approximately 25 by 18 cm at the floor with 5 cm sides. Two to three cross-hatched layers of merino on each face give walls firm enough to hold their shape and a floor dense enough to resist deformation in use.

items.name: Merino roving, contrasting colour for interior (optional)
Foam sheet resist, cut to 20 by 14 cm, 5 mm thick
Olive-oil soap bar or mild wool wash
Hot water (as hot as your hands can bear)

content.text: Method

Step 1: Cut and prepare the resist. Cut a piece of 5 mm foam sheet to 20 by 14 cm. This is the floor area of the tray. Round the corners slightly with scissors so the walls felt smoothly at the corners without pleating sharply. Set the resist aside while you lay out the roving.

Step 2: Lay the first roving face. Place the felting mat on the worktop and lay the tea towel flat on top. Pull thin wisps of roving and lay them over an area of 35 by 25 cm on the towel. The first layer runs horizontally; the second layer runs vertically, crossing the first. Add a third horizontal layer to give the walls enough thickness to hold their shape upright once the resist is removed. Keep the edges of each layer within the 35 by 25 cm zone, as these outer edges will fold over to form the walls.

Step 3: Place the resist. Centre the foam resist on the roving layout so it sits in the middle of the 35 by 25 cm area with approximately 7 to 8 cm of roving extending beyond each long edge and 5 to 6 cm extending beyond each short edge. These overhanging margins will fold over the resist to form the walls and the second face of the floor.

Step 4: Fold the overhanging roving. Fold the roving margins over the edges of the resist, pressing them flat onto the resist's upper face. Where the corners overlap, fold the excess into a neat tuck rather than a pleat where possible. The folded roving now covers the top surface of the resist and forms the inner layer of the floor and the inner face of the walls.

Step 5: Add the second face. Pull thin wisps of roving (the contrasting interior colour if using, or the same main colour) and lay them in two or three cross-hatched layers over the entire upper surface of the resist, covering both the folded margins and the exposed resist area in the centre. This completes the sandwich: roving below the resist, resist in the centre, roving above.

Step 6: Wet and begin felting. Dissolve olive-oil soap in hot water to make a light suds. Pour the soapy water slowly over the top face of the layout, saturating it without displacing the roving. If using a felting net, lay it over the surface before pouring. Rub through the net with flat hands in circular motions for 5 to 6 minutes, keeping the surface hot. Flip the entire sandwich carefully, supporting the resist from below, and repeat the rubbing on the second face for 5 to 6 minutes. Alternate between the two faces every 5 minutes for a total of 20 minutes.

Step 7: Begin fulling. Once the surface holds together when the net is removed (the pinch test shows fibres do not lift away freely), begin fulling by rolling the whole sandwich in the tea towel. Roll back and forth on the felting mat for 3 to 4 minutes, then unroll, turn 90 degrees, and repeat. Continue fulling until the edges of the layout have shrunk noticeably inward and the surface is firm, approximately 20 to 25 minutes of rolling total.

Step 8: Remove the resist. Open the tea towel. Carefully cut a small slit along one short edge of the tray between the two layers, cutting through only one layer of felt. Slide the resist out through the slit. With the resist removed, the walls are now free. Press the tray open with your hands, working the interior corners into a square shape. The walls will fold upright naturally; use your fingers to push them into a consistent 5 cm height all around.

Step 9: Continue fulling the open tray. Work the open tray with hot soapy hands, rubbing the walls and floor firmly for a further 10 to 15 minutes. Pay particular attention to the corners and the top edge of the walls, which need to be worked enough to hold their shape once the tray dries. Throw the tray onto the work surface a few times from a height of 20 to 30 cm to help compact the floor.

Step 10: Rinse and shape. Rinse the tray thoroughly in hot water, then plunge into cold water for 30 to 60 seconds. Press out excess water. Shape the tray by pressing the floor flat on the work surface and pushing the walls upright with your hands. If the walls are splaying outward, place a same-size box or book inside the tray to keep the walls in position while drying. Dry upright away from direct heat.

items.problem: The walls are too soft and collapse when the tray is used
items.cause: Insufficient roving layers in the wall area, or the tray was not fulled long enough after resist removal
items.fix: Three layers of roving in the wall zone is the minimum for a self-supporting wall. If already made, re-wet the tray and work the walls with firm rubbing and throwing for a further 10 minutes, then dry with a support inside.

items.problem: The corners of the tray have thick, puckered seams
items.cause: The folded corner tucks were too bulky or were not worked down sufficiently during the folding step
items.fix: At Step 4, trim corner excess with scissors before folding so that the overlap is minimal. During fulling, work the corners with extra rubbing attention. Once the tray is dry, trim corner lumps lightly with small scissors if needed.

items.problem: The top edge of the walls is uneven in height
items.cause: The roving margins that formed the walls were unequal in width before felting
items.fix: Trim the top edge with sharp scissors after the tray is dry to level it. Measure from the floor at each corner and mark with a fabric pen before cutting. A slight variation in wall height is normal and does not affect function.

items.problem: The two layers of the floor have not bonded at the centre and separate easily
items.cause: The resist was left in too long, or the folded margins were not long enough to cover the resist top fully
items.fix: Re-wet the floor area with hot soapy water and rub the floor from both faces firmly for 10 minutes, pressing the two layers together. This will bond the floor even after the resist is removed, as long as the fibres have not fully compacted without each other.

content.text: Care and finishing

A wet-felted basket tray is hand-wash only. Rinse under cool running water if it becomes marked, press the walls back into shape while wet, and dry upright. Do not machine-wash or tumble-dry; the walls will lose their shape in prolonged agitation. If the floor develops a slight dish after prolonged use, re-wet the floor under warm water, press flat on a hard surface, and dry under a weight. The tray can be lightly trimmed around the top edge with sharp scissors at any point if the surface has any loose wisps or the edge is uneven.
